1577[[1]](#cite_note-yn060121-1)[[2]](#cite_note-it180120-2) – 1578[[1]](#cite_note-yn060121-1)[[2]](#cite_note-it180120-2) [Posthumous promotion](/wiki/Posthumous_promotion "Posthumous promotion") to Chancellor of the Realm (*[Daijō-daijin](/wiki/Daij%C5%8D-daijin "Daijō-daijin")*) in 1582.[[3]](#cite_note-jk190224-3) | |

(織田 信長; [[o.da (|) no.bɯ(ꜜ).na.ɡa, -na.ŋa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Ja-oda_nobunaga.ogg "File:Ja-oda nobunaga.ogg");[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) 23 June 1534 – 21 June 1582) was a Japanese [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") and *[daimyō](/wiki/Daimy%C5%8D "Daimyō")* and one of the leading figures of the [Sengoku](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period") and [Azuchi-Momoyama periods](/wiki/Azuchi-Momoyama_period "Azuchi-Momoyama period"). He was the *Tenka-bito* (天下人; lit. 'person under heaven')[[a]](#cite_note-7) and regarded as the first "Great Unifier" of Japan. He is sometimes referred as the "Demon Daimyō" and "Demon King of the Sixth Heaven".

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Oda_emblem.svg/250px-Oda_emblem.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Oda_emblem.svg)

Oda clan *[mon](/wiki/Mon_(emblem) "Mon (emblem)")* (Japanese emblem)

**Oda Nobunaga** (織田 信長; [[o.da (|) no.bɯ(ꜜ).na.ɡa, -na.ŋa]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Japanese "Help:IPA/Japanese") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Ja-oda_nobunaga.ogg "File:Ja-oda nobunaga.ogg");[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) 23 June 1534 – 21 June 1582) was a Japanese [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") and *[daimyō](/wiki/Daimy%C5%8D "Daimyō")* and one of the leading figures of the [Sengoku](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period") and [Azuchi-Momoyama periods](/wiki/Azuchi-Momoyama_period "Azuchi-Momoyama period"). He was the *Tenka-bito* (天下人; lit. 'person under heaven')[[a]](#cite_note-7) and regarded as the first "Great Unifier" of Japan. He is sometimes referred as the "Demon Daimyō" and "Demon King of the Sixth Heaven".

Nobunaga was an influential figure in Japanese history and is regarded as one of the three great unifiers of Japan, along with his [retainers](/wiki/Kashindan "Kashindan"), [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") and [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu"). Nobunaga paved the way for the successful reigns of Hideyoshi and Ieyasu by consolidating power, as head of the very powerful [Oda clan](/wiki/Oda_clan "Oda clan"), through a series of wars against other *daimyō* beginning in the 1560s. The period when Nobunaga and Hideyoshi were in power is called the [Azuchi–Momoyama period](/wiki/Azuchi%E2%80%93Momoyama_period "Azuchi–Momoyama period"). The name "Azuchi–Momoyama" comes from the fact that Nobunaga's castle, [Azuchi Castle](/wiki/Azuchi_Castle "Azuchi Castle"), was located in [Azuchi, Shiga](/wiki/Azuchi,_Shiga "Azuchi, Shiga"); while [Fushimi Castle](/wiki/Fushimi_Castle "Fushimi Castle"), where Hideyoshi lived after his retirement, was located in Momoyama.[[7]](#cite_note-kotoazuchi-8)

Nobunaga emerged as the most powerful *daimyō*, overthrowing the nominally ruling [shogun](/wiki/Shogun "Shogun") [Ashikaga Yoshiaki](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshiaki "Ashikaga Yoshiaki") and dissolving the [Ashikaga Shogunate](/wiki/Ashikaga_Shogunate "Ashikaga Shogunate") in 1573. He conquered most of [Honshu](/wiki/Honshu "Honshu") by 1580, and defeated the [Ikkō-ikki](/wiki/Ikk%C5%8D-ikki "Ikkō-ikki") in the 1580s. Nobunaga's rule was noted for innovative military tactics, fostering of free trade, reforms of Japan's civil government, and the start of the Momoyama historical art period, but also for the brutal suppression of those who refused to cooperate or yield to his demands.

Nobunaga committed *[seppuku](/wiki/Seppuku "Seppuku")* during the [Honnō-ji Incident](/wiki/Honn%C5%8D-ji_Incident "Honnō-ji Incident") in 1582, when his retainer [Akechi Mitsuhide](/wiki/Akechi_Mitsuhide "Akechi Mitsuhide") ambushed and trapped him in a temple in [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"). Nobunaga was succeeded by Toyotomi Hideyoshi, who along with Tokugawa Ieyasu completed his campaign of national unification shortly afterward.

## Early life (1534–1551)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life (1534–1551)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Portrait_of_Oda_Nobunaga_%28Hideyoshi_Kiyomasa_Memorial_Hall%29_Cropped.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Oda_Nobunaga_%28Hideyoshi_Kiyomasa_Memorial_Hall%29_Cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Oda_Nobunaga_(Hideyoshi_Kiyomasa_Memorial_Hall)_Cropped.jpg)

Portrait of Oda Nobunaga in colour on silk (1583, in Kobe City Museum, Important Cultural Property)

Oda Nobunaga was born on 23 June 1534 in [Nagoya](/wiki/Nagoya "Nagoya"), [Owari Province](/wiki/Owari_Province "Owari Province"), and was the heir of [Oda Nobuhide](/wiki/Oda_Nobuhide "Oda Nobuhide"), the head of the powerful [Oda clan](/wiki/Oda_clan "Oda clan") and a deputy *[shugo](/wiki/Shugo "Shugo")* (military governor), and his lawful wife [Dota Gozen](/wiki/Dota_Gozen "Dota Gozen").[[8]](#cite_note-Jansen-9) Nobunaga was previously thought to have been born in [Nagoya Castle](/wiki/Nagoya_Castle "Nagoya Castle"), but in recent years the theory that he was born in Shobata Castle has become more accepted. Nobunaga was given the childhood name of  *Kippōshi* (吉法師), and through his childhood and early teenage years became well known for his bizarre behavior.

Nobunaga mostly spent his time between the age of 13 (the age of maturity at the time) and 18 in hunting, riding, practicing archery and shooting [arquebus](/wiki/Tanegashima_(gun) "Tanegashima (gun)") (still a novelty in Japan at the time), but also wrestling, swimming, watching [sumo](/wiki/Sumo "Sumo") and visiting taverns and brothels with his friends. He also showed complete disdain for formal clothing and proper social behavior of a lord, wearing sleeveless bathrobes and short trousers tied with hemp rope in public, eating melons while riding backwards on his horse, and often dancing in female clothing in taverns, gaining the nickname *The Fool of Owari*.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-10)[[10]](#cite_note-:1-11)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Nagoyajou1.JPG/250px-Nagoyajou1.JPG)](/wiki/File:Nagoyajou1.JPG)

Site of [Nagoya Castle](/wiki/Nagoya_Castle "Nagoya Castle") (那古野城跡)

Nobunaga was given Nagoya Castle by his father at the age of 8 and lived there for 13 years until he took [Kiyosu Castle](/wiki/Kiyosu_Castle "Kiyosu Castle") at the age of 21. He had one or two older brothers, but they were illegitimate sons. As the first legitimate son, Nobunaga was intended to succeed Nobuhide as leader of the Oda clan, and so he was separated from his mother and given special education. Four [karō](/wiki/Kar%C5%8D "Karō") (chief retainers), [Hayashi Hidesada](/wiki/Hayashi_Hidesada "Hayashi Hidesada"), [Hirate Masahide](/wiki/Hirate_Masahide "Hirate Masahide"), Aoyama Nobumasa, and Naitō Shōsuke (or Katsusuke), were assigned to train and educate him for his future role.[[11]](#cite_note-chunichi20120928-12)

Nobunaga came to manhood and took the name Oda Saburō Nobunaga in 1546. He then led the forces of the Oda clan against rival Kira and Ohama in Mikawa for his first campaign in 1547.

In 1548 or 1549, Nobuhide made peace with [Saitō Dōsan](/wiki/Sait%C5%8D_D%C5%8Dsan "Saitō Dōsan"), lord of [Mino Province](/wiki/Mino_Province "Mino Province") (which had previously been hostile to Owari) through a political marriage between his son Nobunaga and Dōsan's daughter, [Nōhime](/wiki/N%C5%8Dhime "Nōhime"). Nobunaga took Nōhime as his lawful wife, and Dōsan became Nobunaga's father-in-law.[[12]](#cite_note-Turnbull-13) Nobunaga also became involved in government affairs at this time, gaining valuable political experience and insight.

## Unification of Owari (1551–1560)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Unification of Owari (1551–1560)")]

### Succession crisis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Succession crisis")]

Main article: [Battle of Akatsuka](/wiki/Battle_of_Akatsuka "Battle of Akatsuka")

In 1551, Oda Nobuhide died unexpectedly. It has been said that Nobunaga acted outrageously during his funeral, throwing ceremonial [incense](/wiki/Incense "Incense") at the altar.[[13]](#cite_note-tale3-14) Although Nobunaga was Nobuhide's legitimate heir, a succession crisis occurred when some of the Oda clan opposed him. Nobunaga assembled a force of 1,000 men and used them to intimidate and dissuade his enemies, thus preventing a serious disruption in the transfer of power.

Sensing weakness, the *daimyō* [Imagawa Yoshimoto](/wiki/Imagawa_Yoshimoto "Imagawa Yoshimoto") sent an army under the command of [Imagawa Sessai](/wiki/Sessai_Ch%C5%8Dr%C5%8D "Sessai Chōrō") to lay siege to the castle at [Anjō](/wiki/Anj%C5%8D "Anjō"), where [Oda Nobuhiro](/wiki/Oda_Nobuhiro "Oda Nobuhiro"), Nobunaga's older brother, was living. To save his life, Nobunaga was compelled to turn over a hostage held by his clan at Honshōji temple, nine-year-old Matsudaira Takechiyo – later known as [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") – an exchange that helped solidify an alliance between other rival clans. Nobuhiro, frustrated by his low standing in the Oda clan, later plotted against Nobunaga with the assistance of another rival, [Saitō Yoshitatsu](/wiki/Sait%C5%8D_Yoshitatsu "Saitō Yoshitatsu"); Nobunaga learned of the plot but chose to forgive his brother's conduct.

In early 1552, barely several months after his father's death, one of Oda's senior retainers, [Yamaguchi Noritsugu](/w/index.php?title=Yamaguchi_Noritsugu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yamaguchi Noritsugu (page does not exist)")[[jp](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%B1%B1%E5%8F%A3%E6%95%99%E7%B6%99 "jp:山口教継")] and his son [Yamaguchi Noriyoshi](/w/index.php?title=Yamaguchi_Noriyoshi&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yamaguchi Noriyoshi (page does not exist)")[[jp](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%B1%B1%E5%8F%A3%E6%95%99%E5%90%89 "jp:山口教吉")] defected to the Imagawa clan. In response, Nobunaga attacked Noritsugu, but was defeated by Noriyoshi's forces at the [Battle of Akatsuka](/wiki/Battle_of_Akatsuka "Battle of Akatsuka"). Nobunaga retreated and left contested lands in eastern Owari under Imagawa control.[[9]](#cite_note-:0-10)[[10]](#cite_note-:1-11)

### Consolidation of clan leadership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Consolidation of clan leadership")]

Main articles: [Battle of Kiyosu Castle](/wiki/Battle_of_Kiyosu_Castle "Battle of Kiyosu Castle"), [Battle of Muraki Castle](/wiki/Battle_of_Muraki_Castle "Battle of Muraki Castle"), [Battle of Inō](/wiki/Battle_of_In%C5%8D "Battle of Inō"), [Battle of Nagara-gawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Nagara-gawa "Battle of Nagara-gawa"), [Siege of Terabe](/wiki/Siege_of_Terabe "Siege of Terabe"), and [Battle of Ukino](/wiki/Battle_of_Ukino "Battle of Ukino")

In spring 1552, Nobunaga faced a new challenge when his uncle, [Oda Nobutomo](/wiki/Oda_Nobutomo "Oda Nobutomo"), [attacked Nobunaga's domain](/wiki/Battle_of_Kiyosu_Castle#First_Battle "Battle of Kiyosu Castle") with the support of [Shiba Yoshimune](/wiki/Shiba_Yoshimune "Shiba Yoshimune"), governor of [Owari province](/wiki/Owari_province "Owari province"). Nobunaga repelled the attack and burned the outskirts of his uncle's castle at Kiyosu to discourage further attempts. However, Nobutomo was spared any serious punishment.

In 1553, Hirate Masahide, who had been one of Nobunaga's closest advisors and mentors, committed *seppuku*. It is generally believed that he did so to admonish Nobunaga, but the actual motive is unclear.[[b]](#cite_note-15)[[14]](#cite_note-Turnbull2-16): 68 Yoshimune tipped off Nobunaga that Nobutomo planned to assassinate him; Yoshimune was subsequently captured and put to death on Nobutomo's orders. Nobunaga mobilized his forces to [blockade Kiyosu castle](/wiki/Battle_of_Kiyosu_Castle#Second_Battle "Battle of Kiyosu Castle") and set up a lengthy siege.

In 1554, Nobunaga finally achieved victory over the Imagawa clan at the [Battle of Muraki Castle](/wiki/Battle_of_Muraki_Castle "Battle of Muraki Castle"), reclaiming the lands he had lost to them.[[15]](#cite_note-:Chaplin-17)[[16]](#cite_note-:Ōta-18) After securing eastern Owari, Nobunaga then turned his attention back to [the siege at Kiyosu](/wiki/Battle_of_Kiyosu_Castle#Fall_of_Kiyosu_Castle "Battle of Kiyosu Castle"),[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 276 where he eventually defeated Nobutomo and forced him to commit *seppuku*.

In 1556, Saitō Yoshitatsu raised an army against his father, Saitō Dōsan, who was slain in combat at the [Battle of Nagara-gawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Nagara-gawa "Battle of Nagara-gawa"). Nobunaga set out for Oura in Mino with troops to rescue his father-in-law, but immediately withdrew upon hearing of Dōsan's death. Thereafter, Yoshitatsu usurped his father's title and became lord of Mino.[[14]](#cite_note-Turnbull2-16)

The loss of the Saitō clan's support further undermined faith in Nobunaga's leadership; key retainers such as Hayashi Hidesada, Hayashi Michitomo, and Shibata Katsuie soon turned on him. They raised an army to support his brother Nobuyuki (Nobukatsu), who was highly regarded within the Oda clan. Nobunaga defeated the rebels at the [Battle of Ino](/wiki/Battle_of_Ino "Battle of Ino"), but at the plea of his birth mother, Dota Gozen, pardoned them. Despite his brother Michitomo's death in battle, Hidesada pledged his loyalty to Nobunaga and resumed serving him, while Katsuie chose to remain in Nobuyuki's service.

In 1557, however, Nobuyuki conspired with Oda Nobuyasu, lord of Iwakura Castle, to plot another rebellion. Shibata, disgusted by Nobuyuki's treachery and disloyalty, secretly warned Nobunaga. Nobunaga falsely claimed to have fallen ill and had Nobuyuki and his entourage assassinated when they came to visit him. It is said that either [Kawajiri Hidetaka](/wiki/Kawajiri_Hidetaka "Kawajiri Hidetaka") or [Ikeda Tsuneoki](/wiki/Ikeda_Tsuneoki "Ikeda Tsuneoki") carried out Nobuyuki's murder.[[14]](#cite_note-Turnbull2-16): 69

In 1558, Nobunaga sent an army to successfully protect Suzuki Shigeteru, lord of Terabe Castle, during the [Siege of Terabe](/wiki/Siege_of_Terabe "Siege of Terabe"). Shigeteru had defected to Nobunaga's side from Imagawa Yoshimoto, a *daimyō* from [Suruga Province](/wiki/Suruga_Province "Suruga Province") and one of the most powerful men in the Tōkaidō region. In July 1558, he defeated his cousin, Oda Nobukata, deputy governor of northern Owari at [Battle of Ukino](/wiki/Battle_of_Ukino "Battle of Ukino").

By 1559, Nobunaga had captured and destroyed [Iwakura](/wiki/Iwakura,_Aichi "Iwakura, Aichi") Castle, eliminated all opposition within the Oda clan, and established his uncontested rule in Owari Province.[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 276

## Rise to power (1560–1568)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Rise to power (1560–1568)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/Oda_Nobunaga_statue_in_Kiyosu_park.jpg/250px-Oda_Nobunaga_statue_in_Kiyosu_park.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oda_Nobunaga_statue_in_Kiyosu_park.jpg)

Statue of Oda Nobunaga at [Kiyosu Castle](/wiki/Kiyosu_Castle "Kiyosu Castle")

### Conflict with Imagawa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Conflict with Imagawa")]

Main article: [Siege of Marune](/wiki/Siege_of_Marune "Siege of Marune")

Imagawa Yoshimoto was a long-time opponent of Nobunaga's father, and had sought to expand his domain into Oda territory in Owari. In 1560, Imagawa Yoshimoto gathered an army of 25,000 men,[[18]](#cite_note-Takeuchi-20) and marched toward the capital city of [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto"), with the pretext of aiding the frail [Ashikaga Shogunate](/wiki/Ashikaga_shogunate "Ashikaga shogunate"). The [Matsudaira clan](/wiki/Matsudaira_clan "Matsudaira clan") also joined Yoshimoto's forces.

The Imagawa forces quickly overran the border fortresses of Washizu and Matsudaira forces led by [Matsudaira Motoyasu](/wiki/Matsudaira_Motoyasu "Matsudaira Motoyasu") took [Marune Fortress](/wiki/Siege_of_Marune "Siege of Marune") from the Oda clan. Against this, the Oda clan could rally an army of only 2,000 to 3,000 men.[[12]](#cite_note-Turnbull-13)[[19]](#cite_note-21) Some of his advisors suggested that he take refuge at Kiyosu Castle and wait out a siege by the Imagawa, but Nobunaga refused, stating that "only a strong offensive policy could make up for the superior numbers of the enemy", and calmly ordered a counterattack against Yoshimoto.[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19)

#### Battle of Okehazama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Battle of Okehazama")]

Main article: [Battle of Okehazama](/wiki/Battle_of_Okehazama "Battle of Okehazama")

In June 1560, Nobunaga's scouts reported that Yoshimoto was resting at the narrow gorge of Dengaku-Kazama, ideal for a surprise attack, and that the Imagawa army was celebrating their victories over the Washizu and Marune fortresses. While Yoshimoto viewed victory ahead, Nobunaga's forces marched to the [Atsuta Shrine](/wiki/Atsuta_Shrine "Atsuta Shrine"), a fortified temple overlooking the Imagawa camp. Later, Nobunaga moved to [Zensho-ji fort](/w/index.php?title=Zensho-ji_fort&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zensho-ji fort (page does not exist)")[[jp](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%96%84%E7%85%A7%E5%AF%BA%E7%A0%A6 "jp:善照寺砦")], set up a decoy army there, marched rapidly behind Yoshimoto's camp, and attacked after a terrific thunderstorm. Yoshimoto was killed by two Oda samurai.[[20]](#cite_note-22)[[21]](#cite_note-Sato-23) With his victory in this battle, Oda Nobunaga gained greatly in prestige, and many samurai and warlords pledged fealty to him.

[Kinoshita Tōkichirō](/wiki/Kinoshita_T%C5%8Dkichir%C5%8D "Kinoshita Tōkichirō"), who would eventually become Toyotomi Hideyoshi, probably participated in the battle, but nothing is recorded from that time. His exploits were first recorded in the Mino Campaign.

### Alliance with Matsudaira (later Tokugawa) and Takeda

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Alliance with Matsudaira (later Tokugawa) and Takeda")]

Rapidly weakening in the wake of this battle, the [Imagawa clan](/wiki/Imagawa_clan "Imagawa clan") no longer exerted control over the Matsudaira clan. In 1561, an alliance was forged between Oda Nobunaga and Matsudaira Motoyasu (who would become Tokugawa Ieyasu), despite the decades-old hostility between the two clans. Nobunaga also formed an alliance with [Takeda Shingen](/wiki/Takeda_Shingen "Takeda Shingen") through the marriage of his daughter to Shingen's son.[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 277–78[[22]](#cite_note-sam-24)

### Mino campaign

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Mino campaign")]

Main article: [Siege of Inabayama Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Inabayama_Castle "Siege of Inabayama Castle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Tenkahubu.svg/250px-Tenkahubu.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Tenkahubu.svg)

Nobunaga's *Tenka Fubu* seal

In 1561, Saitō Yoshitatsu, Nobunaga's brother-in-law, died suddenly of illness and was succeeded by his son, Nobunaga's nephew, [Saitō Tatsuoki](/wiki/Sait%C5%8D_Tatsuoki "Saitō Tatsuoki"). Yoshitatsu murdered his father and brothers to become daimyō, and Nobunaga had attempted to avenge the murder of his father-in-law numerous times. Nobunaga's nephew Tatsuoki was young and much less effective as a ruler and military strategist than his father and grandfather.[[12]](#cite_note-Turnbull-13): 57 Taking advantage of this situation, Nobunaga moved his base to [Komaki Castle](/wiki/Komaki "Komaki") and started his campaign in Mino Province, defeating Tatsuoki in both the [Battle of Moribe](/w/index.php?title=Battle_of_Moribe&action=edit&redlink=1 "Battle of Moribe (page does not exist)")[[14]](#cite_note-Turnbull2-16): 216and the [Battle of Jushijo](/w/index.php?title=Battle_of_Jushijo&action=edit&redlink=1 "Battle of Jushijo (page does not exist)") in June that same year.

By convincing Saitō retainers to abandon their incompetent and foolish master, Nobunaga significantly weakened the [Saitō clan](/wiki/Sait%C5%8D_clan "Saitō clan"). In 1564, Oda Nobunaga dispatched his retainer, [Kinoshita Tōkichirō](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi"), to bribe many of the warlords in the Mino area to support the Oda clan. In 1566, Nobunaga charged Kinoshita with building [Sunomata Castle](/wiki/Sunomata_Castle "Sunomata Castle") on the bank of the [Sai River](/wiki/Sai_River_(Gifu) "Sai River (Gifu)") opposite Saitō territory to serve as a staging point for the Oda forces and to intimidate, surprise, and demoralize the enemy.

In 1567, the [Mino Triumvirate](/wiki/Mino_Triumvirate "Mino Triumvirate") (西美濃三人衆, Nishi-Mino Sanninshū)--three samurai generals, [Inaba Ittetsu](/wiki/Inaba_Ittetsu "Inaba Ittetsu"), [Andō Michitari](/wiki/And%C5%8D_Michitari "Andō Michitari"), and [Ujiie Bokuzen](/wiki/Ujiie_Bokuzen "Ujiie Bokuzen"), who served the Saitō clan--agreed to change sides and join the forces of Oda Nobunaga. Their combined forces mounted a victorious final attack at the [Siege of Inabayama Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Inabayama_Castle "Siege of Inabayama Castle").[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 278

After taking possession of the castle, Nobunaga changed the name of both [Inabayama Castle](/wiki/Gifu_Castle "Gifu Castle") and the surrounding town to [Gifu](/wiki/Gifu,_Gifu "Gifu, Gifu"). Nobunaga derived the term *Gifu* from the legendary Mount Qi (岐山 *Qi* in [Standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese")) in [China](/wiki/China "China"), on which the [Zhou dynasty](/wiki/Zhou_dynasty "Zhou dynasty") is fabled to have started. Nobunaga revealed his ambition to conquer the whole of Japan and also started using a new personal [seal](/wiki/Seal_(emblem) "Seal (emblem)") that read **Tenka Fubu** (天下布武),[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 278[[23]](#cite_note-omi-25) literally "[All under heaven](/wiki/All_under_heaven "All under heaven"), spreading military force", or more idiomatically, "All the world by force of arms". Remains of Nobunaga's residence in Gifu can be found today in [Gifu Park](/wiki/Gifu_Park "Gifu Park").[[24]](#cite_note-map-26)

### Ise campaign, Omi campaign, and march to Kyoto

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Ise campaign, Omi campaign, and march to Kyoto")]

Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(November 2025)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Main articles: [Siege of Chōkō-ji](/wiki/Siege_of_Ch%C5%8Dk%C5%8D-ji "Siege of Chōkō-ji") and [Ashikaga Shogunate](/wiki/Ashikaga_Shogunate "Ashikaga Shogunate")

Following Nobunaga's conquest of Mino Province in 1567, Nobunaga sent [Takigawa Kazumasu](/wiki/Takigawa_Kazumasu "Takigawa Kazumasu") on a campaign comprising two invasions of [Ise Province](/wiki/Ise_Province "Ise Province") in 1567 and 1568 that defeated numerous families of Ise (Ise was ruled nominally by the [Kitabatake clan](/wiki/Kitabatake_clan "Kitabatake clan") and [Kanbe clan](/w/index.php?title=Kanbe_clan&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kanbe clan (page does not exist)")). After Nobunaga controlled Ise, his son, [Oda Nobutaka](/wiki/Oda_Nobutaka "Oda Nobutaka") was installed as the head of the Kanbe clan. Later in 1569, head of Kitabatake clan, [Kitabatake Tomonori](/wiki/Kitabatake_Tomonori "Kitabatake Tomonori"), adopted Nobunaga's second son [Oda Nobukatsu](/wiki/Oda_Nobukatsu "Oda Nobukatsu").

Nobunaga also arranged for [Oichi](/wiki/Oichi "Oichi"), his sister, to marry rival warlord [Azai Nagamasa](/wiki/Azai_Nagamasa "Azai Nagamasa") from [Omi Province](/wiki/Omi_Province "Omi Province") in an effort to cement an alliance. Nobunaga desired peaceful relations with the [Azai clan](/wiki/Azai_clan "Azai clan") because of their strategic position between the Oda clan's land and the capital, Kyoto.

In 1568, [Ashikaga Yoshiaki](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshiaki "Ashikaga Yoshiaki") and [Akechi Mitsuhide](/wiki/Akechi_Mitsuhide "Akechi Mitsuhide"), as Yoshiaki's bodyguard, went to [Gifu](/wiki/Gifu "Gifu") to ask Nobunaga to start a campaign toward Kyoto. Yoshiaki was the brother of the murdered 13th shogun of the Ashikaga Shogunate, [Yoshiteru](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshiteru "Ashikaga Yoshiteru"), who had been killed by the Miyoshi *tannins* (three chiefs of the [Miyoshi clan](/wiki/Miyoshi_clan "Miyoshi clan"), [Miyoshi Nagayuki](/w/index.php?title=Miyoshi_Nagayuki&action=edit&redlink=1 "Miyoshi Nagayuki (page does not exist)"), [Miyoshi Masayasu](/w/index.php?title=Miyoshi_Masayasu&action=edit&redlink=1 "Miyoshi Masayasu (page does not exist)") and [Iwanari Tomomichi](/wiki/Iwanari_Tomomichi "Iwanari Tomomichi")). Yoshiaki wanted revenge against the killers who had already set up a puppet shogun, [Ashikaga Yoshihide](/wiki/Ashikaga_Yoshihide "Ashikaga Yoshihide").

Nobunaga agreed to install Yoshiaki as the new shogun and, grasping the opportunity to enter Kyoto, started his campaign. An obstacle in southern Ōmi Province was the [Rokkaku clan](/wiki/Rokkaku_clan "Rokkaku clan"), led by [Rokkaku Yoshikata](/wiki/Rokkaku_Yoshikata "Rokkaku Yoshikata"), who refused to recognize Yoshiaki as shogun and was ready to go to war to defend Yoshihide. In response, Nobunaga launched a rapid attack on Chōkō-ji Castle, driving the Rokkaku clan out of their castles.[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 278–79 (Later in 1570, the Rokkaku tried to [retake the castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Ch%C5%8Dk%C5%8D-ji "Siege of Chōkō-ji"), but they were driven back by Oda forces led by [Shibata Katsuie](/wiki/Shibata_Katsuie "Shibata Katsuie").) Other forces led by [Niwa Nagahide](/wiki/Niwa_Nagahide "Niwa Nagahide") defeated the Rokkaku on the battlefield and entered [Kannonji Castle](/wiki/Kannonji_Castle "Kannonji Castle"), before resuming Nobunaga's march to Kyoto.

The approaching Oda army influenced the [Matsunaga clan](/wiki/Matsunaga_clan "Matsunaga clan") to submit to the future shogun. The *daimyō* [Matsunaga Hisahide](/wiki/Matsunaga_Hisahide "Matsunaga Hisahide") kept his title by making this decision to ally his clan with Oda and the new shogun.

On 9 November 1568, Nobunaga entered Kyoto, drove out the [Miyoshi clan](/wiki/Miyoshi_clan "Miyoshi clan"), who had supported the 14th shogun and who fled to [Settsu](/wiki/Settsu "Settsu"), and installed Yoshiaki as the 15th shogun of the Ashikaga Shogunate. However, Nobunaga refused the title of shogun's deputy ([Kanrei](/wiki/Kanrei "Kanrei")), or any appointment from Yoshiaki, even though Nobunaga had great respect for the [Emperor Ōgimachi](/wiki/Emperor_%C5%8Cgimachi "Emperor Ōgimachi").[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 279–81[[25]](#cite_note-27)

## Unification of Japan (1568–1582)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Unification of Japan (1568–1582)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cf/Oda-Nobunaga-by-Utagawa-Kuniyoshi.png/250px-Oda-Nobunaga-by-Utagawa-Kuniyoshi.png)](/wiki/File:Oda-Nobunaga-by-Utagawa-Kuniyoshi.png)

[Ukiyo-e](/wiki/Ukiyo-e "Ukiyo-e") of Oda Nobunaga by [Kuniyoshi Utagawa](/wiki/Kuniyoshi_Utagawa "Kuniyoshi Utagawa")

### Conflict with Asakura, Ashikaga and Azai

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Conflict with Asakura, Ashikaga and Azai")]

After installing Yoshiaki as shogun, Nobunaga forced Yoshiaki to call all *daimyō* to come to Kyoto and attend the court banquet. [Asakura Yoshikage](/wiki/Asakura_Yoshikage "Asakura Yoshikage"), head of the [Asakura clan](/wiki/Asakura_clan "Asakura clan") and regent of Ashikaga Yoshiaki, refused, which prompted Nobunaga to declare Asakura Yoshikage as a rebel.

#### Siege of Kanegasaki

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Siege of Kanegasaki")]

Main article: [Siege of Kanegasaki (1570)](/wiki/Siege_of_Kanegasaki_(1570) "Siege of Kanegasaki (1570)")

In early 1570, Nobunaga then raised an army and marched on the Asakura clan's domain in [Echizen](/wiki/Echizen_Province "Echizen Province").[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 281 Nobunaga besieged [Kanagasaki Castle](/wiki/Kanagasaki_Castle "Kanagasaki Castle"). This action created a conflict between Nobunaga and shogun Ashikaga Yoshiaki. Yoshiaki secretly started an "anti-Nobunaga alliance", conspiring with other *daimyō* to get rid of Nobunaga. Azai Nagamasa, to whom Nobunaga's sister Oichi was married, broke the alliance with the Oda clan to honor the Azai-Asakura alliance, which had lasted for three generations.

With the help of the Rokkaku clan, Miyoshi clan, and the [Ikkō-ikki](/wiki/Ikk%C5%8D-ikki "Ikkō-ikki"), the anti-Nobunaga alliance sprang into full force, taking a heavy toll on the Oda clan. After Nobunaga found himself facing both the Asakura and Azai forces and when defeat looked certain, Nobunaga decided to [retreat from Kanagasaki](/wiki/Siege_of_Kanegasaki_(1570) "Siege of Kanegasaki (1570)"), which he did successfully.

#### Battle of Anegawa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Battle of Anegawa")]

Main article: [Battle of Anegawa](/wiki/Battle_of_Anegawa "Battle of Anegawa")

In July 1570, the Oda-[Tokugawa](/wiki/Tokugawa_clan "Tokugawa clan") allies laid siege to [Yokoyama Castle](/w/index.php?title=Yokoyama_Castle&action=edit&redlink=1 "Yokoyama Castle (page does not exist)") and [Odani Castle](/wiki/Odani_Castle "Odani Castle") in Ōmi Province. Later, the combined Azai-Asakura force marched out to confront Nobunaga. Nobunaga advanced to the southern bank of the [Anegawa River](/wiki/Anegawa_River "Anegawa River").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The following morning, 30 July 1570, the battle began. Tokugawa Ieyasu joined his forces with Nobunaga, with the Oda and Azai clashing on the Anegawa east river while Tokugawa and Asakura grappled on the west of the river. The battle turned into a melee fought in the middle of the shallow Anegawa River.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

For a time, Nobunaga's forces fought the Azai upstream, while the Tokugawa warriors fought the Asakura downstream. After the Tokugawa forces finished off the Asakura, they turned and hit the Azai's right flank. The troops of the Mino Triumvirate, who had been held in reserve, then came forward and hit the Azai left flank. Soon both the Oda and Tokugawa forces defeated the combined forces of the Asakura and Azai clans.[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 282

#### End of Asakura and Azai clan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: End of Asakura and Azai clan")]

Main articles: [Siege of Ichijodani castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Ichijodani_castle "Siege of Ichijodani castle") and [Siege of Odani](/wiki/Siege_of_Odani "Siege of Odani")

1573 represented the end of Azai and Asakura clans, as Nobunaga successfully destroyed the Azai and Asakura clans by driving them both to the point that their clan leaders committed suicide.[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 281, 285–86[[22]](#cite_note-sam-24): 156 After [destroying Ichijōdani Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Ichij%C5%8Ddani_Castle "Siege of Ichijōdani Castle"), the castle home of Asakura Yoshikage, then pursuing Yoshikage to the Rokubō-kenshō monastery, where Yoshikage killed himself, Nobunaga then finished off the Azai clan at the [Siege of Odani Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Odani_Castle "Siege of Odani Castle").

Main article: [Ikkō-ikki](/wiki/Ikk%C5%8D-ikki "Ikkō-ikki")

Nobunaga faced a significant threat from the Ikkō-ikki, a resistance movement centered around the [Jōdo Shinshū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo_Shinsh%C5%AB "Jōdo Shinshū") sect of [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"). The Ikkō-ikki began as a cult association for self-defense, but popular antipathy against the [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") due to the constant violence of the [Sengoku period](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period") caused their numbers to swell. By the time of Nobunaga's rise to power, the Ikkō-ikki was a major organized armed force opposed to samurai rule in Japan.

In August 1570, Nobunaga launched the [Ishiyama Hongan-ji War](/wiki/Ishiyama_Hongan-ji_War "Ishiyama Hongan-ji War") against the Ikkō-ikki, while simultaneously fighting against his samurai rivals. In May 1571, Nobunaga besieged [Nagashima](/wiki/Nagashima "Nagashima"), a series of Ikkō-ikki fortifications in Owari Province, beginning the [Sieges of Nagashima](/wiki/Sieges_of_Nagashima "Sieges of Nagashima"). However, Nobunaga's first siege of Nagashima ended in failure, as his trusted general Shibata Katsuie was severely wounded and many of his samurai were lost before retreating. Despite this defeat, Nobunaga was inspired to launch another siege, the [Siege of Mount Hiei](/wiki/Siege_of_Mount_Hiei "Siege of Mount Hiei").

#### Siege of Mount Hiei

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Siege of Mount Hiei")]

Main article: [Siege of Mount Hiei](/wiki/Siege_of_Mount_Hiei "Siege of Mount Hiei")

The [Enryaku-ji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji") temple on [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei") was an issue for Nobunaga. The monastery's *sōhei* ([warrior monks](/wiki/S%C5%8Dhei "Sōhei")) of the [Tendai school](/wiki/Tendai "Tendai") were aiding his opponents in the Azai-Asakura alliance and the temple was close to his base of power. In September 1571, Nobunaga preemptively attacked the Enryaku-ji temple, then besieged Mount Hiei and razed it.

In the process of making their way to the Enryaku-ji temple, Nobunaga's forces destroyed and burnt all buildings, killing monks, [laymen](/wiki/Laymen "Laymen"), women, and children and eliminating anyone who had previously escaped their attack. It is said that "The whole mountainside was a great slaughterhouse and the sight was one of unbearable horror."[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 284

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Oda_Nobunaga_armour.jpg/250px-Oda_Nobunaga_armour.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oda_Nobunaga_armour.jpg)

Oda Nobunaga's [armour](/wiki/Japanese_armour "Japanese armour")

#### Fall of Nagashima

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Fall of Nagashima")]

Main article: [Siege of Nagashima](/wiki/Siege_of_Nagashima "Siege of Nagashima")

In July 1573, after the successful siege of Mount Hiei, Nobunaga besieged Nagashima a second time, personally leading a sizable force with many [arquebusiers](/wiki/Arquebus "Arquebus"). However, a rainstorm rendered his arquebuses inoperable while the Ikkō-ikki's own arquebusiers could fire from covered positions. Nobunaga himself was almost killed and forced to retreat, with the second siege being considered his greatest defeat.

In 1574, Nobunaga launched a third siege of Nagashima as his general [Kuki Yoshitaka](/wiki/Kuki_Yoshitaka "Kuki Yoshitaka") began a naval blockade and bombardment of Nagashima, allowing him to capture the outer forts of Nakae and Yanagashima as well as part of the Nagashima complex. The sieges of Nagashima finally ended when Nobunaga's men completely surrounded the complex and set fire to it, killing the remaining tens of thousands of defenders and inflicting tremendous losses to the Ikkō-ikki.[[14]](#cite_note-Turnbull2-16): 221–25

#### Fall of Ishiyama Hongan-ji

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Fall of Ishiyama Hongan-ji")]

Main article: [Ishiyama Hongan-ji War](/wiki/Ishiyama_Hongan-ji_War "Ishiyama Hongan-ji War")

Simultaneously, Nobunaga had been besieging the Ikkō-ikki's main stronghold at [Ishiyama Hongan-ji](/wiki/Ishiyama_Hongan-ji "Ishiyama Hongan-ji") in present-day [Osaka](/wiki/Osaka "Osaka"). Nobunaga's [Siege of Ishiyama Hongan-ji](/wiki/Siege_of_Ishiyama_Hongan-ji "Siege of Ishiyama Hongan-ji") began to slowly make some progress, but the Mōri clan of the [Chūgoku region](/wiki/Ch%C5%ABgoku_region "Chūgoku region") broke his naval blockade and started sending supplies into the strongly fortified complex by sea. As a result, in 1577, Nobunaga ordered Takigawa Kazumasu to suppress Ikko-ikki at [Kii Province](/wiki/Kii_Province "Kii Province"), [Hashiba Hideyoshi](/wiki/Hashiba_Hideyoshi "Hashiba Hideyoshi") to conquer the Chūgoku region from the Mori clan, before advancing upon the Mori clan in [Nagato Province](/wiki/Nagato_Province "Nagato Province"),[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 287, 306 Akechi Mitsuhide to pacify [Tanba Province](/wiki/Tanba_Province "Tanba Province"), Kuki Yoshitaka to support attack from the sea, and Nobunaga eventually blocked the Mōri's supply lines.[[14]](#cite_note-Turnbull2-16): 228[[17]](#cite_note-Sansom2-19): 288–89

In 1580, ten years after the siege of Ishiyama Hongan-ji began, the son of Chief Abbot [Kōsa](/wiki/K%C5%8Dsa "Kōsa") surrendered the fortress to Nobunaga after their supplies were exhausted, and they received an official request from the Emperor to do so.[[26]](#cite_note-28) Nobunaga spared the lives of Ishiyama Hongan-ji's defenders but expelled them from Osaka and burnt the fortress to the ground. Although the Ikkō-ikki continued to make a last stand in [Kaga Province](/wiki/Kaga_Province "Kaga Province"), Nobunaga's capture of Ishiyama Hongan-ji crippled them as a major military force.

### Conflict with Takeda

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Conflict with Takeda")]

Main articles: [Siege of Iwamura Castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Iwamura_Castle "Siege of Iwamura Castle") and [Battle of Mikatagahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Mikatagahara "Battle of Mikatagahara")

One of the strongest rulers in the anti-Nobunaga alliance was Takeda Shingen, who had formerly been an ally of the Oda clan. At the apex of the anti-Nobunaga coalition, in 1572, Takeda Shingen ordered [Akiyama Nobutomo](/wiki/Akiyama_Nobutomo "Akiyama Nobutomo"), one of the "[Twenty-Four Generals](/wiki/Twenty-Four_Generals_of_Takeda_Shingen "Twenty-Four Generals of Takeda Shingen")" of Shingen, to [attack Iwamura castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Iwamura_Castle "Siege of Iwamura Castle"). Nobunaga's aunt, [Lady Otsuya](/wiki/Lady_Otsuya "Lady Otsuya"), conspiring against the Oda clan, surrendered the castle to the Takeda, and married Nobutomo. Nobunaga was shocked by Shingen's betrayal, and his rage was immense. His letter to [Uesugi Kenshin](/wiki/Uesugi_Kenshin "Uesugi Kenshin") was filled with angry words, stating, "Shingen knows nothing of a samurai's honor," "This grudge will never cease," and "I will never reconcile with Shingen, now or in the future."[[27]](#cite_note-29)

In the same year, Shingen decided to make a drive for Kyoto at the urging of the shogun Ashikaga Yoshiaki, starting with invading Tokugawa territory. Nobunaga, tied down on the western front, sent lackluster aid to Tokugawa Ieyasu, who suffered defeat at the [Battle of Mikatagahara](/wiki/Battle_of_Mikatagahara "Battle of Mikatagahara") in early 1573. However, after the battle, Tokugawa's forces launched night raids and convinced Takeda of an imminent counter-attack, thus saving the vulnerable Tokugawa with the bluff. This would play a pivotal role in Tokugawa's philosophy of strategic patience in his campaigns with Nobunaga. Shortly thereafter, the Takeda forces were neutralized after Shingen died in April 1573.[[22]](#cite_note-sam-24): 153–56

#### Battle of Nagashino

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Battle of Nagashino")]

Main article: [Battle of Nagashino](/wiki/Battle_of_Nagashino "Battle of Nagashino")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Battle-of-Nagashino-Map-Folding-Screen-1575.png/250px-Battle-of-Nagashino-Map-Folding-Screen-1575.png)](/wiki/File:Battle-of-Nagashino-Map-Folding-Screen-1575.png)

[Battle of Nagashino](/wiki/Battle_of_Nagashino "Battle of Nagashino") in 1575

In 1575, [Takeda Katsuyori](/wiki/Takeda_Katsuyori "Takeda Katsuyori"), son of Takeda Shingen, moved to Tokugawa territory, [attacked Yoshida castle](/wiki/Siege_of_Yoshida_Castle "Siege of Yoshida Castle") and later besieged [Nagashino Castle](/wiki/Nagashino_Castle "Nagashino Castle"). Katsuyori, angered when [Okudaira Sadamasa](/wiki/Okudaira_Sadamasa "Okudaira Sadamasa") rejoined the Tokugawa, had originally conspired with [Oga Yashiro](/wiki/Oga_Yashiro "Oga Yashiro") to take the Tokugawa-controlled [Okazaki Castle](/wiki/Okazaki_Castle "Okazaki Castle"), the capital of [Mikawa Province](/wiki/Mikawa_Province "Mikawa Province"). This plot failed.[[28]](#cite_note-battles-30): 80–82 Tokugawa Ieyasu appealed to Nobunaga for help and Nobunaga personally led an army of about 30,000 men to the relief of Nagashino Castle. The combined force of 38,000 men under Oda Nobunaga and Tokugawa Ieyasu devastated the Takeda clan at the Battle of Nagashino, the greatest defeat of the Takeda clan.

Conventionally, the "Battle of Nagashino" was regarded as a historic defeat in which Takeda Katsuyori ordered his cavalry to charge recklessly into a horse guard fence where arquebusiers were waiting for them, losing many Takeda officers and soldiers. Moreover, it has been said that Nobunaga developed a new battle strategy called "three-stage shooting", in which arquebusiers were arranged in several rows with the front row firing a volley, and then making way for the second row to fire. Once the second row had fired and made way for the third row, the first row had reloaded and were ready to fire again. This way the Oda could keep a relatively steady rate of musket fire.[[29]](#cite_note-31)

However, this was a theory developed by the Imperial Japanese Army General Staff based on Oze Hoan's Shinchō Ki and Tōyama Nobuharu's Sōken Ki, which are war chronicles. Later, as research based on documents, letters, and Ota Gyūichi's *Shinchō Kōki* progressed, many errors were pointed out. It is now believed that it was mainly the logistics in Nobunaga's hands that determined the winner.[[c]](#cite_note-32)[[30]](#cite_note-president70430-33)[[31]](#cite_note-newsweekjapan101942-34)

#### End of Takeda clan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: End of Takeda clan")]

Main articles: [Siege of Takatō (1582)](/wiki/Siege_of_Takat%C5%8D_(1582) "Siege of Takatō (1582)") and [Battle of Tenmokuzan](/wiki/Battle_of_Tenmokuzan "Battle of Tenmokuzan")

The end of the Takeda clan came in 1582 when [Oda Nobutada](/wiki/Oda_Nobutada "Oda Nobutada") and Tokugawa Ieyasu forces conquered [Shinano](/wiki/Shinano_Province "Shinano Province") and [Kai Province](/wiki/Kai_Province "Kai Province"). Takeda Katsuyori was defeated at the [Battle of Tenmokuzan](/wiki/Battle_of_Tenmokuzan "Battle of Tenmokuzan") and then committed suicide.

### End of the Ashikaga Shogunate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oda_Nobunaga&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: End of the Ashikaga Shogunate")]
